short.headline: India trades more than ever, but still imports more than it exports
short.dek: From $1.3 bn in 1948 to $445 bn in exports by 2025, India's trade has multiplied. But a persistent goods deficit of $308 bn is offset by services and remittances.
short.body: India's merchandise trade has grown a hundredfold since Independence. In 2025, exports hit US$ 445.28 billion and imports US$ 753.48 billion, leaving a goods deficit of US$ 308.2 billion. Services now earn a surplus of US$ 138.85 billion, and remittances add another US$ 137.67 billion, softening the external imbalance. Measured against GDP, exports and imports together now account for about 44.7% of the economy. On a per-person basis, however, India's trade is still just US$ 800 a year. The current account deficit, the broadest measure, stood at 0.8% of GDP in 2024.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, macha?
macha.body: Think of India like a house that buys more groceries than it sells in the neighbourhood shop. In 2025, we bought US$ 753 bn worth of goods from the world and sold only US$ 445 bn. That's a gap of US$ 308 bn, or ₹25 lakh crore at today's exchange rates. But the house has other income: Indian tech workers and businesses sell services worth US$ 139 bn more than they import, and the diaspora sends home another US$ 138 bn. These inflows mostly plug the hole. So overall, India's external account is slightly in the red, a manageable 0.8% of GDP. The big picture: India's trade is huge and growing fast, but it still depends on the world for things like oil and electronics, and on its people abroad to send money home.
macha.soWhat: India's trade deficit is large but largely covered by service exports and remittances, leaving the country in a stable external position.

article.title: How does India trade with the world?
article.standfirst: A journey through India's merchandise trade, services, remittances and the current account, told in ten essential charts.
article.bodyMarkdown:
India's commerce with the world has been transformed over seven decades. The numbers are vast, but the patterns are clear: India sells far less goods than it buys, services are a rising strength, and money sent home by Indians abroad is a lifeline. Here is the full story, chart by chart.

## How much does India trade, and how did it get here?

In 1948, India's merchandise exports were a modest US$1.3 billion. By 2025, they had climbed to US$445.28 billion. Imports grew even faster, from US$1.43 billion to US$753.48 billion. The chart shows both lines rising almost together, but with imports pulling away decisively after the 2000s. Before 1991, trade grew sluggishly. Liberalisation, then the 2000s global boom, and a surge in oil and electronics imports pushed the lines skyward. Today, India is a major trading nation, but the goods deficit is structural: it has widened from a negligible minus US$0.13 billion in 1948 to minus US$308.2 billion in 2025. The takeoff is recent, two-thirds of this trade volume was added in just the last twenty years.

## Why does India buy more goods than it sells?

India's merchandise trade balance has been negative every year since 1948, with only a brief exception in the early 1970s. The deficit has ballooned from near zero to US$308.2 billion in 2025. Why? One visible reason in this data is India's heavy import dependence on crude oil, gold, and electronic goods, while its exports, petroleum products, gems and jewellery, engineering goods, have not kept pace. The deficit is not necessarily bad; it means India is investing and consuming more than it earns from goods sales. But it also means the country must find other ways to pay, which is where services and remittances come into the picture.

## Is India really that open? Comparing trade to GDP

Dollar numbers can mislead because the whole economy has expanded. Trade as a share of GDP tells us how open India really is. In 1960, exports of goods and services were just 4.5% of GDP and imports 6.8%. By 2024, exports had risen to 21.2% and imports to 23.5%. That is more than a fourfold increase in openness. The chart shows a sharp climb after 1991, a leap in the mid-2000s, and a plateau after the global financial crisis. Even so, India's trade-to-GDP ratio peaked around 55% in 2011-12 and has since fallen back, reflecting both slower global trade growth and a rising domestic economy. For the common reader, this means roughly one in every five rupees spent in India flows across a border.

## Is India becoming a bigger part of world trade?

India's share of world merchandise exports is a revealing number. In 1948, it was 2.2%. Seventy-seven years later, it is 1.7%. That seems like a decline, but it masks a U-shaped story. The share fell to a low of 0.5% in the early 1990s as other Asian economies raced ahead, then recovered slowly to 1.7% by 2025. The trend since 2000 is upward, but India still accounts for a small slice of global goods exports, roughly one in every sixty dollars of world merchandise trade. Services exports are a brighter spot, where India's share is larger, but for goods, the climb has been modest.

## How much of export 'growth' is just higher prices?

Export value can rise because you sell more, or because prices go up. The chart separates the two. In 1980, both the value index and the volume index started near 3.2 and 5.1 (2015=100). By 2025, the nominal value index had reached 166.2 while the real volume index stood at 141.7. The gap is the difference: part of the 'growth' is just inflation or higher global prices. But real volumes have still multiplied by nearly 28 times since 1980. So while inflation matters, India genuinely ships a lot more stuff today than it did forty years ago.

## How much does trade matter for a person in India?

The headline totals look giant, but India has 1.45 billion people. Trade per person, exports plus imports of goods, was just US$8 in 1960. By 2024, it had reached US$800. That is a hundredfold rise, but in absolute terms it remains modest. For comparison, the world average is several thousand dollars. The chart makes clear that India's trade integration is still shallow at the individual level. Even as container ports bustle and export parks hum, the typical Indian buys or sells only a few hundred dollars worth of goods with the rest of the world each year.

## Who fills the gap left by the goods deficit?

The goods deficit of US$308.2 billion in 2025 does not tell the whole story. This chart brings in three flows: goods balance, services balance, and remittances. The red goods line plunges deeper into deficit. But the green services line shows a growing surplus, reaching US$138.85 billion in 2025, thanks to IT, business services, and consultancy exports. The blue remittance line brings another US$137.67 billion (2024 data). Together, services surplus and remittances covered almost 90% of the goods deficit in 2024-25. Without them, India's external position would look far more precarious.

## What happens when we add services to goods trade?

Combine merchandise and commercial services, and the net trade picture improves but remains in deficit. The net trade balance (goods + services) stood at minus US$169.36 billion in 2025. That is roughly half the goods-only deficit. The line has fluctuated between minus US$121 billion and minus US$169 billion over the last fifteen years, with no clear trend. Services have prevented the combined deficit from exploding along with the goods deficit. Still, even with services included, India spends more than it earns from international trade in goods and services combined.

## Why are remittances so important for India?

India is the world's top recipient of personal remittances. As a share of GDP, they have grown from 0.4% in 1975 to 3.5% in 2024. In dollar terms, that is US$137.67 billion flowed in from overseas Indians in 2024. The chart traces a steady climb, with a jump after the 1990s Gulf migration boom and another surge in the 2000s as the Indian diaspora expanded into skilled professions worldwide. Remittances are now larger than the services surplus, and they provide a stable, counter-cyclical inflow, often rising when the home economy slows. For millions of families, this is the money that pays for housing, education, and emergencies.

## Is India living within its external means?

The current account balance is the fullest measure: it captures trade in goods and services, remittances, and income from investments. Since 1975, India has run a deficit in most years. The latest reading, for 2024, is minus 0.8% of GDP, small by historical standards. Deficits of 3-4% in 2011-12 prompted worries, but today's gap is manageable. The line has seesawed, with rare surpluses in 2001-04 and again briefly in 2020. A current account deficit is not inherently good or bad; it means India invests more than it saves, with the rest financed from abroad. The chart shows that, overall, India has mostly balanced its external books within a narrow band, thanks to the cushion of services and remittances.

editorialPlan.heroDescription: India's journey from a tiny, closed trading nation to a global player with a large goods deficit, a booming services surplus, and a vital remittance lifeline. The data shows explosive growth in merchandise trade, a structural import bill, and how services and diaspora money keep the current account manageable.

pullQuotes.quote: India's goods deficit hit US$308.2 billion in 2025, a record.
Remittances at 3.5% of GDP are now larger than the entire agriculture sector's share of the economy.
pullQuotes.numberLabel: Remittances as a share of GDP, latest

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Imagine your household budget: every month you earn money from your job (exports) and spend on groceries, rent, and fuel (imports). The difference is your 'trade balance'. For a country, the merchandise trade balance is exports of physical goods minus imports of physical goods. If the number is negative, you bought more than you sold, a deficit. A deficit is not automatically bad; it can mean you are investing or consuming more. But it does mean you need to borrow or earn from other sources to pay for it.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: India runs a large merchandise trade deficit every year; understanding this number is key to the whole page.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of a country like a person who has a salary (exports), buys things (imports), receives gifts from relatives abroad (remittances), and earns interest on savings (investment income). The current account adds all these up. If the final number is negative, the country spent more than it earned from the rest of the world and must borrow or sell assets to cover the gap. A small deficit can be fine; a large one can be a warning signal.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The current account is the ultimate summary of how India is managing its external finances, the page's concluding chart.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: When a person working in Dubai or London sends money back to their family in Kerala or Punjab, that is a remittance. It counts as an inflow for India because it brings foreign currency into the country. Remittances are not earnings from selling goods or services; they are transfers from individuals. For India, they are huge, the world's largest recipient, and they act like a stable financial cushion.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Remittances play a starring role in balancing India's external account, as several charts show.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Per capita simply means 'per person'. If India's total trade is US$1 trillion, but there are 1.4 billion people, then trade per capita is about US$700. Dividing by population makes the number relatable. It shows whether the average person's life is deeply linked to international trade or not. For India, per capita trade is still modest, meaning many citizens are not directly part of the global trading system.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The trade-per-person chart adds an essential reality check to the giant headline figures.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Suppose you sell ten crates of mangoes every year. If the price of mangoes doubles, your sales value doubles even though you sold the same number of crates. The nominal value is the money you received; the real volume is the actual crates. In trade statistics, a 'real' index strips out inflation to show physical quantity changes, while a 'nominal' index includes price changes. This distinction helps you avoid being fooled by rising prices alone.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The real vs nominal export growth chart helps the reader understand whether India's export 'boom' is truly about shipping more goods or just about inflation.

visualizationDecisions.reason: Long history shows the transformation clearly on a phone screen when zoomed out.
A line chart best highlights the widening deficit trend.
Two lines on the same scale show the rise and convergence clearly.
The U-shaped pattern over decades is clearest on a line.
Side-by-side lines make the gap between volume and value obvious.
A steady upward line is easy to read; full history shows how far we've come.
Three distinct series on a common dollar scale; the crossing paths tell the story.
One combined deficit series is simple and effective.
Shows the rising importance as a share of the economy.
A percentages-of-GDP line around zero is the clearest way to see whether the deficit is stable.

chartExplainers.title: How much India trades, 1948 to today
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's merchandise trade has exploded from under US$1.5 billion in 1948 to US$1.2 trillion in 2025, with imports consistently outpacing exports.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart shows exports and imports of merchandise goods since Independence. In 1948, both were tiny, exports US$1.3 billion and imports US$1.43 billion. For decades, lines crept upward together. After 1991, they steepen. The 2000s bring a sharp acceleration: by 2025, exports are US$445.28 billion and imports US$753.48 billion. Notice how imports surpass exports and the gap widens dramatically after 2005. This gap, the merchandise trade deficit, is the central feature of India's external trade.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It is the essential headline chart that answers 'How much does India trade?' with scale and history.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Watch the blue export line and the orange import line. The space between them grows wider over time, showing the deficit.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume the axes start at zero; the chart begins at 1948 values of around US$1 billion, not zero.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A dual-line chart with sparse date ticks and a prominent final-year callout works well on mobile.

chartExplainers.title: India's goods trade deficit
chartExplainers.takeaway: The merchandise trade deficit widened from near zero in 1948 to a record US$308.2 billion in 2025.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart isolates the merchandise trade balance, the dollar value of goods exports minus goods imports. It stays negative almost all years, but the scale is what matters. Until the late 1990s, the deficit rarely crossed US$10 billion. Then it balloons: US$100 billion around 2011, US$200 billion by 2018, and US$308.2 billion in 2025. The line plunges downward in the last decade, reflecting India's heavy import bill for oil, electronics, and gold. The deficit is structural: it has grown alongside the economy, showing no sign of reversal.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It zeroes in on the single most talked-about number in India's trade story, the goods deficit.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A line below the zero axis is a deficit; the deeper the line drops, the larger the deficit.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't interpret a larger deficit in dollar terms as automatically worse if the economy and exports are also growing fast.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A single declining line with clear negative values and a bold final marker is easy to scan.

chartExplainers.title: How open India's economy is
chartExplainers.takeaway: Trade as a share of GDP rose from under 5% in 1960 to over 44% by 2024, making India a significantly more open economy.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart scales trade against the size of the economy. Exports (goods and services) as a percentage of GDP went from 4.5% in 1960 to 21.2% in 2024; imports from 6.8% to 23.5%. The lines move together, peaking around 2011-12 and then edging down. The gap between imports and exports is the trade gap as a share of GDP, which has been about 2-4% recently. The chart reveals that even though absolute trade numbers are massive, trade is not as dominant as in some smaller export-oriented economies. India's domestic market is still the main driver.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It contextualizes the raw dollar volumes, showing that trade has indeed become more important to India's economy.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Higher lines mean trade is more important relative to GDP. The gap between them is the trade balance as a share of GDP.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't mistake a falling ratio for declining trade; it often means GDP grew faster than trade.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A multi-line chart with % left axis and clear 2024 labels works well on mobile.

chartExplainers.title: India's slice of world exports
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's share of world merchandise exports declined from 2.2% in 1948 to 1.7% in 2025, but has been slowly recovering since the 1990s.
chartExplainers.detail: In the late 1940s, India accounted for over 2% of world goods exports, a legacy of colonial trade networks. By the early 1990s, it had fallen to just 0.5% as other Asian economies surged. Since then, the share has climbed back to 1.7% in 2025. The line shows a long U-shape: decline, bottom, slow recovery. Even with this recovery, India remains a relatively small player in global goods exports, roughly one-sixtieth of the world total. The story for services is brighter, but for goods, the climb has been modest.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides the most direct measure of India's standing in global trade, stripping out India's own inflation and growth.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A U-shaped line with a low point in the early 1990s; recent values are higher but still below the 1948 start.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse share of world exports with total exports; share can fall even when exports rise if world trade grows faster.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A single clear line with a few labelled milestones (1948, 1991, 2025) is mobile-friendly.

chartExplainers.title: Real vs nominal export growth
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's nominal export value index rose to 166.2 by 2025, while the real volume index reached 141.7, showing inflation accounts for part of the growth.
chartExplainers.detail: Both indices start near zero in 1980 (2015=100). By 2025, the nominal value index (which includes price changes) is 166.2, while the real volume index (which strips out price changes) is 141.7. The difference, about 24.5 index points, is the inflation effect. Both lines rise together, but after 2000 they begin to separate, especially during commodity price spikes. This tells you that India's export growth is not just prices, real volumes have still multiplied by nearly 28 times since 1980. But it also warns that a chunk of the dollar 'record' is simply higher global prices.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It debunks the notion that all export growth is real; it helps readers see through inflation.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Two lines that diverge over time; the vertical gap at any point shows the price effect.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't assume the value line shows true growth; always compare to the volume line.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Two distinct lines with a clear legend and a callout for the 2025 gap are mobile-suitable.

chartExplainers.title: India's trade per person
chartExplainers.takeaway: Trade per capita grew from just US$8 in 1960 to US$800 in 2024, but remains modest by global standards.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart divides total merchandise trade (exports + imports) by India's population. In 1960, the average Indian's trade footprint was tiny: US$8 per year. By 2024, it had risen to US$800, a hundredfold increase. The line climbs steadily, with acceleration after the 1990s. Yet US$800 per person still means the typical Indian is not deeply plugged into global trade. Compare this to the world average, which is several thousand dollars. The chart underscores a key fact: India's trade numbers are big because its population is huge, not because every citizen is a global trader.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It personalizes the trade story and prevents the reader from being overwhelmed by billions and trillions.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A gently rising line over 64 years; note the final value of US$800.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't compare this directly to GDP per capita; trade per capita measures a flow, not income.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple upward line with a final pop-out of US$800 works well on a small screen.

chartExplainers.title: Who pays for the goods deficit?
chartExplainers.takeaway: Services surplus of US$138.85 billion and remittances of US$137.67 billion together covered nearly 90% of the goods deficit in 2024-25.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart puts three flows on the same dollar scale: the merchandise trade balance (deep red deficit), the commercial services balance (green surplus), and personal remittances (blue). Since 2010, the goods deficit has grown from around US$100 billion to over US$300 billion. The services surplus has widened from US$2.4 billion to US$138.85 billion, driven by IT and business services. Remittances have risen from US$46 billion to US$137.67 billion. Together, these two positive flows almost cancel out the goods deficit. The chart makes visually obvious that without services and diaspora money, India's external position would be in serious trouble.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It tells the complete story of how India finances its goods deficit, tying the earlier charts together.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Three lines: one negative (below zero), two positive. Watch how the positive lines rise to meet the negative one.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't think remittances and services are the same; remittances are transfers, services are earnings from trade.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A three-line chart with clearly labeled series and a zero line works if colors are distinct.

chartExplainers.title: The net trade balance
chartExplainers.takeaway: Even after adding services surplus, India's combined goods-and-services trade balance was still in deficit at US$169.36 billion in 2025.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart adds merchandise and commercial services trade together to get a single net trade balance. Since 2010, the combined balance has fluctuated between minus US$121 billion and minus US$169 billion. Despite the growing services surplus, the net deficit remains large and has not improved much over the last decade. It was minus US$121.52 billion in 2010 and minus US$169.36 billion in 2025. The line shows that services have merely contained the deficit, not eliminated it. The combined trade gap has widened in recent years as merchandise imports outpace service export growth.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides a truer picture of trade than merchandise alone, and sets up the need for remittances.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A single line below zero; lower means larger deficit. Note it remains stubbornly negative.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't interpret a stable deficit as 'no problem'; a growing deficit in dollar terms even at a stable % of GDP can accumulate debt.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple deficit line with a clear Y-axis showing negative billions and a data point for 2025 is readable on mobile.

chartExplainers.title: The remittance lifeline
chartExplainers.takeaway: Remittances as a share of GDP have risen from 0.4% in 1975 to 3.5% in 2024, underlining their growing macroeconomic weight.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart tracks personal remittances received as a percentage of GDP. In 1975, they were just 0.4% of GDP. The line begins to climb in the 1990s, crossing 2% in the early 2000s and reaching 3.5% in 2024. In dollar terms, that is US$137.67 billion. The chart shows that remittances are not only large but also have grown faster than the economy itself for most of this period. They now exceed the combined value of India's electronic goods exports or its total agricultural exports, making them a critical component of external stability. For many states like Kerala, Goa, and Punjab, remittances are an economic backbone.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It visualizes the often-overlooked role of diaspora money in India's macroeconomy.
chartExplainers.howToRead: An upward line with a % Y-axis. Notice the rise accelerates after 1990 and again after 2020.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't confuse remittances as a share of GDP with total remittance dollars; GDP-based share adjusts for economic size.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A clean line chart with % values and a few callout years is easy to digest on phone.

chartExplainers.title: The external balance
chartExplainers.takeaway: India's current account deficit stood at a manageable 0.8% of GDP in 2024, far below the near-5% deficits of 2012-13.
chartExplainers.detail: The current account balance, as a percentage of GDP, sums up trade in goods and services, remittances, and investment income. The chart shows India has run a deficit in most years since 1975, typically between 0% and 3% of GDP. It spiked to about -4.8% in 2012-13, triggering a taper tantrum, but has since moderated. The 2024 value of -0.8% is comfortably low. The line oscillates around the zero mark, with rare surpluses in the early 2000s and briefly in 2020. This tells us that, overall, India's external sector is in balance, services and remittances ensure the deficit never runs out of control.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It is the ultimate scoreboard for India's external economic health, drawing all threads together.
chartExplainers.howToRead: A fluctuating line hovering around zero. When it dips below zero, India runs a deficit. The magnitude shows vulnerability.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't treat a small deficit as a sign of crisis; many growing economies run modest current account deficits.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A line chart with a prominent zero line and labelled peaks and troughs works well on mobile.

sectionVisualMap.heading: How much does India trade, and how did it get here?
Why does India buy more goods than it sells?
Is India really that open? Comparing trade to GDP
Is India becoming a bigger part of world trade?
How much of export 'growth' is just higher prices?
How much does trade matter for a person in India?
Who fills the gap left by the goods deficit?
What happens when we add services to goods trade?
Why are remittances so important for India?
Is India living within its external means?

sourceNotes: WTO trade statistics for merchandise exports, imports, and volume/value indices, compiled and derived for this page.
World Bank data on exports and imports of goods and services as a percentage of GDP, personal remittances received, and current account balance.
Trade per capita is derived as total merchandise trade divided by population, using WTO and UN population estimates.
Services trade balance and net goods+services balance are derived from WTO and IMF data.
Remittances as a share of GDP is derived using World Bank GDP data.
caveats: Merchandise trade data extends from 1948 to 2025; services and remittance data start later (2010 and 1975, respectively).
Trade per capita is a rough measure and does not capture informal trade, smuggling, or digital services accurately.
The current account balance includes not only trade and remittances but also investment income, which is reported with a lag and subject to revision.
All values are in current US dollars unless specified as a share of GDP; exchange rate movements affect comparability across years.
The world export share is based on WTO merchandise data and may differ slightly from other sources.
lockedNumbersUsed: Exports of goods and services, latest
Exports of goods and services, earliest
Imports of goods and services, latest
Imports of goods and services, earliest
India's share of world merchandise exports, latest
India's share of world merchandise exports, earliest
Remittances as a share of GDP, latest
Remittances as a share of GDP, earliest
qualityFlags: Trade per capita uses population estimates that may have small errors but does not affect the overall trend.
Services trade data before 2010 is not available in the derived dataset, limiting long-run comparison.
The current account as a percentage of GDP uses annual GDP in current dollars, which can be volatile due to exchange rates.

theme.label: Economy and public money
readerQuestions: What is the headline economic number?
Is this a total number or a per-person number?
How much of the story is prices, exchange rates, or GDP share?
What does this explain about household life?

plannedCharts.title: How much India trades, 1948 to today
plannedCharts.subtitle: WTO · merchandise exports and imports · current US$
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline: the scale of India's goods trade and how recently it took off.

plannedCharts.title: India's goods trade deficit
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India buys more goods than it sells, and the gap has widened.

plannedCharts.title: How open India's economy is
plannedCharts.subtitle: World Bank · trade as a share of GDP
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Trade matters more to India than the raw dollars suggest, measured against the size of the economy.

plannedCharts.title: India's slice of world exports
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single clearest measure of India's rise as a trading nation.

plannedCharts.title: Real vs nominal export growth
plannedCharts.subtitle: WTO · merchandise export value vs volume index
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: How much of India's export 'growth' is real, and how much is just higher prices.

plannedCharts.title: India's trade per person
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The per-person reality check behind the big aggregate numbers.

plannedCharts.title: Who pays for the goods deficit?
plannedCharts.subtitle: WTO + World Bank · annual flows with the world
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The deficit story is incomplete without the two flows that fund it.

plannedCharts.title: The net trade balance
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Goods and services combined, a truer picture than goods alone.

plannedCharts.title: The remittance lifeline
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India is the world's largest recipient of remittances, money sent home by its diaspora.

plannedCharts.title: The external balance
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Whether India, overall, lives within its external means.

sourceSummaries.title: India's share of world merchandise exports
Remittances as a share of GDP

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
